lens.included_activities: Private lower-middle-market owners and operators of crude-oil pipeline systems or segments that repeatedly transport crude oil for external shippers under tariffs or transportation contracts.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive producer or refiner pipelines without meaningful external customers, passive non-control infrastructure interests, construction contractors, terminal-only businesses outside pipeline transportation, natural-gas and refined-products pipelines, and entities whose operations or permits cannot transfer with control.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Recurring transportation charges paid by crude producers, marketers, refiners, and other shippers under indexed or otherwise regulated tariffs, negotiated contracts, throughput commitments, and related capacity arrangements.

a.bridge: May 2023 BLS OEWS directly measures the industry's occupation mix but not task exposure: production occupations are 28.27% of employment, petroleum pump-system operators/refinery operators/gaugers 19.20%, transportation/material-moving occupations 22.53%, maintenance/repair 7.74%, and business/financial operations 5.51%. PHMSA documents remote SCADA control and computational pipeline monitoring. I expose portions of alarm triage, monitoring analysis, scheduling, reporting, integrity-data review, maintenance planning, compliance documentation, and commercial administration while preserving accountable control, inspection, repair, field response, and safety decisions. Occupational employment is not wage-weighted task time, and higher-paid analytical roles may bias wage-weighted exposure above employment-share intuition.
a.rationale: The industry already produces structured operational data and uses remote monitoring, making anomaly analysis, document review, scheduling, reporting, and decision support technically plausible. Physical maintenance and transportation work remains, and safety-critical controller decisions cannot be treated as unattended generic automation.
a.change_evidence: A wage-weighted task inventory from crude-pipeline operators, linking controller logs, alarm volumes, integrity-management hours, engineering reviews, maintenance work orders, regulatory reporting, scheduling, and back-office time to payroll, would materially tighten the range.
caveats: OEWS occupation categories do not measure AI exposure or current automation.
Some analytical work is already automated by SCADA and computational monitoring and is therefore excluded from not-yet-automated opportunity.
The frozen compensation-to-receipts ratio uses 2024 wages over 2022 receipts and is harmonized to the IPS basis as stated in the assignment.

rho.bridge: PHMSA shows that hazardous-liquid control rooms already use regulated SCADA systems and that electronic leak detection ranges from simple balances to sophisticated computational models, with trained controllers still interpreting outputs and acting. I use this as evidence of deployable digital infrastructure, then discount exposed tasks for validation, cybersecurity, alarm-management, auditability, safety-case, integration, and change-control constraints. Large regulated operators may have better data than LMM firms but also stricter approval burdens, so the net direction of error is uncertain.
rho.rationale: AI assistance can be implemented in maintenance planning, integrity-data review, document preparation, scheduling, and lower-risk control-room analysis. Safety-critical actions, abnormal-condition response, and regulatory accountability require conservative human-in-the-loop implementation, limiting realized substitution within five years.
rho.change_evidence: Audited before-and-after deployments at small crude-pipeline operators showing labor hours, false alarms, leak-detection sensitivity, controller workload, maintenance backlog, report-cycle time, cyber findings, and regulator acceptance would materially move the estimate.
caveats: Existing computational monitoring is not necessarily AI and may leave less incremental opportunity than greenfield comparisons suggest.
The source evidence establishes feasibility and constraints, not a measured five-year implementation share.
A safety incident or cyber event could materially slow deployment.

e.rationale: The target industry inherently provides recurring transportation, but many pipeline systems are captive affiliates, joint ventures, passive infrastructure interests, or too dependent on a parent producer, refinery, or single shipper to satisfy the independent transferable-firm lens. The already small frozen LMM count makes each classification consequential.
e.change_evidence: A legal-entity and asset map of the seven frozen LMM-band firms showing ownership, external-shipper revenue, tariff status, throughput commitments, permits, operating responsibility, related-party volumes, and change-of-control provisions would directly measure eligibility.
caveats: No source measures lens eligibility among LMM crude-pipeline firms.
NAICS establishments may sit inside larger integrated energy groups and need not correspond to transferable firms.
The frozen count of seven firms is a margin-bridged estimate, not an observed EBITDA-band census.

s5.bridge: Gallup's fall-2024 US survey found that 22% of employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years. I apply that cross-industry employer-business rate to eligible LMM crude-pipeline firms and widen for infrastructure asset sales, joint-venture changes, regulatory approvals, contract consents, and the difference between plans and completed control transfers. Asset-level deal activity could make transfers more frequent, but ownership complexity and failure to qualify as firm control can bias the other way.
s5.rationale: The cross-industry anchor is preferable to assuming transfer activity from visible large midstream deals. A qualifying transfer must convey operational control of a viable external-service business, not merely a passive interest or internal affiliate restructuring.
s5.change_evidence: Five years of closed and failed transactions involving small crude-pipeline operators, coded for change of control, asset-only versus firm sale, related-party status, regulatory approval, and continuing operations, would materially change the estimate.
caveats: Gallup covers employer businesses across industries and sizes rather than pipeline operators.
Stated owner plans are not completed transfers and include gifts or rare public offerings.
The tiny eligible population makes the realized five-year proportion highly discrete.

q.rationale: FERC's predominant indexed price-cap method lets interstate oil pipelines charge up to rate ceilings tied to industry-wide cost changes rather than each operator's immediate realized cost, so firm-specific efficiency can be retained. Shipper negotiations, contract renewals, rate complaints, cost-of-service alternatives, competitive routes, and lower throughput can still share or erase part of the benefit.
q.change_evidence: Pipeline-level tariff, contract, Form 6 cost, complaint, renewal, and realized-revenue data before and after automation, including ship-or-pay commitments and utilization, would directly reveal five-year pass-through.
caveats: FERC reports that approximately 86% of interstate crude and petroleum-product pipeline rates use indexing, but the target includes intrastate and negotiated arrangements.
Rate ceilings are not necessarily realized rates, particularly where pipelines compete or have spare capacity.
The estimate excludes implementation and demand effects.

d5.bridge: PHMSA reports crude-oil pipeline mileage declining from 83,209 miles in 2024 to 81,829 in 2025, while EIA AEO2026 projects US crude production decreasing through the mid-2030s in nearly all cases but refinery throughput staying above 14.7 million barrels per day and crude exports remaining substantial. I translate those asset, supply, refining, and trade indicators into a five-year constant-quality transport-quantity ratio centered on modest decline. Miles are capacity rather than throughput, and national production can diverge from transported barrels because of imports, exports, basin shifts, utilization, and gathering classifications; error direction is uncertain.
d5.rationale: Near-term production pressure and recent mileage contraction argue against strong aggregate growth, while stable refinery throughput, exports, long asset lives, and basin-specific constraints support durable volumes. Individual small systems can diverge sharply when wells decline or a committed shipper changes routes.
d5.change_evidence: A five-year basin-by-basin forecast of crude pipeline throughput, committed minimum volumes, refinery receipts, export-terminal flows, capacity additions and retirements, and shipper production decline curves for the eligible systems would materially move the estimate.
caveats: Pipeline mileage is not constant-quality service quantity.
EIA projections span wide supply cases and are national rather than specific to LMM systems.
Energy policy, oil prices, drilling productivity, refinery closures, exports, and basin bottlenecks can shift rapidly.

o.rationale: Almost all remaining year-five crude pipeline quantity requires an accountable asset operator to maintain integrity, control flows, manage SCADA and alarms, inspect and repair facilities, respond to abnormal conditions, secure the system, and comply with PHMSA and rate obligations. AI may change staffing and decision support but cannot supply physical pipeline transportation as software alone.
o.change_evidence: Regulatory approvals and operating records for fully remote or autonomous pipeline systems, including staffing minima, control-center consolidation, intervention rates, inspection robotics, maintenance outsourcing, and liability assignment, would test the small displacement allowance.
caveats: No source directly estimates a five-year operator-required share.
The accountable operator could be consolidated into a larger platform even though the physical service remains operator-required.
Demand destruction is represented in d5 and is not counted again here.

narrative.executive_view: Crude-oil pipelines are highly automated physical infrastructure with meaningful data, compliance, engineering, and control-room workflows still performed by people. AI can improve analysis and administration, but low labor intensity, strict safety accountability, and a tiny pool of potentially independent LMM operators constrain the opportunity.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can prioritize alarms and integrity findings, summarize inspections, identify anomalous trends, improve maintenance planning, draft regulatory documentation, support scheduling and nominations, and accelerate contract or invoice review. Controllers, engineers, integrity managers, and field technicians remain responsible for validation, abnormal conditions, repairs, emergency response, and regulated decisions.
narrative.value_capture: Indexed rate ceilings can allow a pipeline to retain firm-specific operating savings because ceilings move with industry costs rather than immediately resetting to each operator's cost. Negotiated shipper contracts, rate challenges, competitive alternatives, and renewal leverage reduce retention, especially on underutilized systems.
narrative.firm_availability: The frozen universe contains only seven estimated LMM-band firms, and a substantial share may be captive, affiliated, jointly owned, or economically inseparable from one producer or refiner. A viable control transfer requires operating responsibility, external customers, permits, contracts, and integrity obligations to move together.
narrative.demand_durability: Pipeline assets remain essential for transported barrels, but EIA's current outlook points to near-term US crude-production decline in most cases and PHMSA reports recent crude-pipeline mileage contraction. Refinery throughput and exports remain substantial, so aggregate quantity is more likely to decline modestly than disappear, with large basin-level variation.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: Safety incidents, leaks, cybersecurity events, integrity remediation, environmental liability, shipper concentration, basin decline, regulatory changes, tariff disputes, and stranded capacity can overwhelm administrative savings. Evidence on LMM ownership and transferability is particularly weak. The industry would be unattractive where the pipeline is captive, underutilized, exposed to a single declining shipper, or carries unbounded integrity liabilities.
narrative.principal_driver: Data-rich regulated operations support AI-assisted monitoring, integrity analysis, maintenance planning, and compliance while indexed rate ceilings can preserve savings.
narrative.principal_weakness: Only a handful of estimated LMM firms exist, many may be captive or nontransferable, and physical safety obligations sharply limit labor substitution.

sources.title: Pipeline Transportation of Crude Oil — May 2023 OEWS Industry-Specific Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates
sources.supports: Direct industry occupation mix, including production occupations at 28.27%, petroleum pump-system operators/refinery operators/gaugers at 19.20%, transportation/material-moving at 22.53%, maintenance/repair at 7.74%, and business/financial operations at 5.51% of employment.

sources.title: Control Room Management
sources.supports: PHMSA states that pipeline safety regulations prescribe requirements for controllers, control rooms, and SCADA systems used to remotely monitor and control hazardous-liquid pipelines, including human-factor reliability and abnormal-condition response.

sources.title: Fact Sheet: Leak Detection Systems
sources.supports: Describes electronic leak detection from volume and mass balances to rate-of-change and computational pipeline monitoring, while noting that trained controllers or dispatchers use the systems to locate and isolate leaks.

sources.title: Annual Report Mileage for Hazardous Liquid or Carbon Dioxide Systems
sources.supports: Reports US crude-oil pipeline mileage of 81,829 in 2025, down from 83,209 in 2024 and 84,149 in 2023.

sources.title: Five-Year Review of the Oil Pipeline Index, Docket RM26-6-000
sources.supports: Explains indexed price-cap regulation and states that roughly 350 interstate oil pipelines file tariffs and approximately 86% of crude-oil and petroleum-product transportation rates are set under indexing, with negotiated contracts also referencing the index.

sources.title: North American Industry Classification System — Sector 48-49
sources.supports: Defines NAICS 486110 as establishments primarily engaged in pipeline transportation of crude oil and distinguishes natural-gas and refined-product pipelines.

sources.title: Most Small-Business Owners Lack a Succession Plan
sources.supports: Fall-2024 US survey found that 22% of employer-business owners planned to sell or transfer ownership within five years.

sources.title: Hazardous Liquid Integrity Management
sources.supports: PHMSA describes operator duties to identify, prioritize, assess, evaluate, repair, and validate integrity for hazardous-liquid pipelines that could affect high-consequence areas.

sources.title: Annual Energy Outlook 2026 Narrative
sources.supports: Projects US crude production decreasing through the mid-2030s in nearly all cases; refinery throughput remains above 14.7 million barrels per day in all cases; crude exports remain material, with wide supply-case uncertainty.
